claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

Could you tell me:
1. **How old is your child?** (or if you have multiple children, please tell me their ages)
2. **What are their names?** (This helps me keep track if you have more than one child)

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **20 or over** generally don't qualify for Child Benefit

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm inquiring about my two children.

I have Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 18.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with Alex (17) and Blake (18).

Since both of your children are between 16 and 19 years old, their eligibility will depend on whether they're in approved education or training. Let me check the requirements for this age group.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can continue if they meet certain conditions. Let me ask you about each child:

**For Alex (age 17):**
- Is Alex currently in approved education or training?
- Or has Alex recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)?
- Or neither of these?

**For Blake (age 18):**
- Is Blake currently in approved education or training?
- Or has Blake recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)?
- Or neither of these?

Please let me know the situation for each child, and I'll guide you through the specific requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Sure, let me tell you about each of them.

**Alex** is still in education - he's studying for his A-levels at sixth form.

**Blake** has finished education and isn't studying anymore. He left school a while back, so it's been more than a few months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me work through each child's situation:

**For Alex (17, studying A-levels at sixth form):**

Since Alex is currently in education, I need to check if his A-level course meets the requirements. Let me navigate to the education level check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news for Alex! A-levels are specifically listed as approved (non-advanced) education. Now I need to check a few more details about his course:

**For Alex's A-level course:**
1. Is it a **full-time course** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is this course **funded or arranged by an employer**, or leading to a job already arranged with an employer?

Most sixth form A-level courses are full-time and not employer-funded, so Alex likely qualifies, but I need to confirm these details.

**For Blake (18, left education more than a few months ago):**

Since Blake left education "a while back" and it's been more than a few months, he's likely outside the 20-week extension period that applies after leaving education. Let me get the exact time limit to confirm.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see the extension period is **20 weeks** after leaving education. 

**Can you tell me more specifically when Blake left education?** If it's been more than 20 weeks (about 5 months), then unfortunately Blake would no longer qualify for Child Benefit.

In the meantime, please let me know about Alex's course details so I can continue with his assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
**For Alex:**
Yes, it's a full-time A-level course at sixth form - definitely more than 12 hours a week. And no, it's not employer-funded or anything like that. It's just regular sixth form education.

**For Blake:**
He left school well over 5 months ago, so yes, it's definitely been more than 20 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Let me work through each child's eligibility:

---

## **For Alex (17, full-time A-levels at sixth form):**

Perfect! Alex's A-level course meets the requirements:
- ✓ A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education
- ✓ Full-time (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded

Let me continue checking Alex's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check: **Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

For children aged 16+, they can't be working 24 or more hours per week to qualify for Child Benefit.

---

## **For Blake (18, left education over 5 months ago):**

Since Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, he would no longer qualify under the extension period. Let me confirm this is the correct outcome for his situation. Blake is not currently in education or training, and the extension period has expired, so unfortunately **Blake does not qualify** as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

Please let me know about Alex's work situation, and I'll complete the assessment for him.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
**For Alex:**
No, Alex doesn't work at all. He's just focused on his A-levels right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with Alex's assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question for Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right**, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Income Support

Most 17-year-olds in sixth form don't receive these benefits, but I need to confirm. Is Alex receiving any of these benefits in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in his own name. He's just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! One more question about Alex's education:

**Is Alex doing an apprenticeship?** You mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form, so I'm assuming the answer is no, but I need to confirm because apprenticeships have different rules depending on the region.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship. He's just doing regular A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is looking good so far. Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check your (the claimant's) eligibility. The first requirement is about residency:

**Do you normally live in the UK?** 

If you do, that satisfies the residency requirement. 

(There are also special circumstances that can qualify you if you have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or if you're a Crown servant living abroad, but most claimants simply live in the UK normally.)

What's your situation regarding UK residency?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now regarding your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** This means either:
- You live with Alex, OR
- You contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards Alex's upkeep (food, clothes, housing, etc.)

You mentioned Alex is "living at home with me," so I'm assuming you live together. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** 

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you would need to agree with them about transferring the claim to you.

What's the situation - is anyone else claiming for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check Alex's living situation. You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, but I need to confirm there are no special circumstances:

**Is Alex:**
- In hospital (for an extended period)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Alex is just living normally at home with me. No hospital, no care, no fostering.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question for Alex:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

Since Alex lives at home with you and is just a regular sixth form student, I'm assuming the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. He just lives at home with me and I support him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! Let me now provide you with the complete assessment for both of your children:

---

## **ELIGIBILITY ASSESSMENT RESULTS**

### **Alex (17, studying A-levels at sixth form): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

Alex qualifies as a qualifying child for Child Benefit because:
- ✓ Age 17 (between 16-19)
- ✓ In approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form)
- ✓ Full-time course (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✓ Not receiving benefits in own right
- ✓ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✓ You live with and are responsible for Alex
- ✓ No one else is claiming for Alex
- ✓ Living normally at home
- ✓ Council not paying for accommodation/maintenance

**You ARE eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

---

### **Blake (18, left education over 5 months ago): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit because:
- Age 18 and not currently in approved education or training
- Left education more than 20 weeks ago (the extension period has expired)

For 16-19 year olds who are not in education, Child Benefit can only continue for up to 20 weeks after they leave education. Since Blake left over 5 months ago, this extension period has passed.

**You are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.**

---

## **NEXT STEPS FOR ALEX:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit:**
   - Apply online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Proof of Alex's identity
   - Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate

3. **Payment information:**
   - Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

4. **Important note about High Income:**
   - If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge applies
   - This is a tax charge that increases until income reaches £80,000
   - You can still claim Child Benefit but may need to repay some or all of it through tax
   - You can choose to opt out of receiving payments but still register (this protects National Insurance credits)

---

**Do you have any questions about these results or the application process?**

reasonings: Alex (age 17) is eligible for Child Benefit. He meets all qualifying criteria: he is between 16-19 years old and enrolled in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form) on a full-time basis (12+ hours per week). The course is not employer-funded. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant meets residency requirements (lives in Manchester, UK), has responsibility for Alex (lives together), no other person is claiming for Alex, Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital/care/fostering), and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria under the Child Benefit rules are satisfied.

Blake (age 18) is not eligible for Child Benefit. While he is under 20 years old, he is not currently in approved education or training. Blake left education over 5 months ago (more than 20 weeks). For 16-19 year olds who are not in education, Child Benefit can only continue for up to 20 weeks after leaving education as an extension period. Since Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, he no longer qualifies as a qualifying child under the Child Benefit rules. He does not meet the education/training requirement necessary for 16-19 year olds.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). Alex is eligible for Child Benefit as he is in approved full-time education (A-levels) and meets all other qualifying criteria. Blake is not eligible because he left education over 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training, meaning he no longer qualifies under the 16-19 age category rules. The claimant can claim Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 17) is eligible for Child Benefit. He meets all qualifying criteria: he is between 16-19 years old and enrolled in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form) on a full-time basis (12+ hours per week). The course is not employer-funded. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant meets residency requirements (lives in Manchester, UK), has responsibility for Alex (lives together), no other person is claiming for Alex, Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital/care/fostering), and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria under the Child Benefit rules are satisfied.

Blake (age 18) is not eligible for Child Benefit. While he is under 20 years old, he is not currently in approved education or training. Blake left education over 5 months ago (more than 20 weeks). For 16-19 year olds who are not in education, Child Benefit can only continue for up to 20 weeks after leaving education as an extension period. Since Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, he no longer qualifies as a qualifying child under the Child Benefit rules. He does not meet the education/training requirement necessary for 16-19 year olds.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). Alex is eligible for Child Benefit as he is in approved full-time education (A-levels) and meets all other qualifying criteria. Blake is not eligible because he left education over 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training, meaning he no longer qualifies under the 16-19 age category rules. The claimant can claim Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.
